title: Port meets starboard on the beat

q1.prompt: You're on port, wanting the right side. A converges on starboard — the cross is 50/50. What do you do?

options.label: Hold course and cross her
Bear away and duck her stern
Tack onto starboard now
Hail for room and keep going

answer.explanation: Rule 10: port keeps clear of starboard, and a 50/50 cross is no cross. Duck her stern and keep sailing toward the side you want — a panic tack parks you in her bad air. There is no room to hail for in open water.
answer.cost: Force A to alter course and you owe a Two-Turns Penalty (Rule 44.2); contact adds Rule 14.

title: Sagging down onto a leeward boat

q1.prompt: Same tack, overlapped: A is on your leeward bow, holding a high line. You're slowly sagging down on her. What do you do?

options.label: Trim on and climb clear to windward
Hold your lane — she'll live
Bear away across her stern for speed
Hail her to keep clear

answer.explanation: Rule 11: windward keeps clear of leeward, and you are the windward boat. Sag into her space and you're in the wrong the moment she has to respond. Climb off her line or tack out — she owes you nothing here.
answer.cost: If A must luff or bear away to avoid you: Two-Turns Penalty on you.

title: Catching the boat ahead on the run

q1.prompt: Downwind, you're faster and closing on A from clear astern. What do you do?

options.label: Pick a side and pass keeping clear
Sail straight over her transom line
Hail her to move over
Come in close to leeward and luff her

answer.explanation: Rule 12: clear astern keeps clear of clear ahead — being faster earns you nothing. Set up a passing lane to one side; if you go leeward, remember the overlap brings new limits (Rules 15 and 17). She has no obligation to make space for you.
answer.cost: Bump her transom or force a course change: Two-Turns Penalty on you.

title: She tacks onto your lee bow

q1.prompt: A crossed you on port and tacked right on your lee bow. She's just reached close-hauled. What do you do?

options.label: Hold course — she must give you room at first
Bear away hard at her transom in protest
Luff instantly to head to wind
Tack away immediately — you have no rights

answer.explanation: While tacking she had to keep clear of you; the moment she reached close-hauled she became the leeward boat with right of way (Rule 11). But Rule 15 says a boat that takes right of way by her own action must initially give you room to keep clear. So hold briefly, then respond — you're not required to anticipate her.
answer.cost: If she tacked so close you couldn't respond, she owes the penalty; if you had room and sat on her, you do.

title: She rolls in to leeward at the leeward mark

q1.prompt: You reached the zone clear ahead. Now A has rolled in from astern, overlapped on your outside (leeward) side. What do you do?

options.label: Keep clear of her, but take your room and round
Give her the inside — she's leeward now
Luff her away from the mark
Go wide and let her through

answer.explanation: You were clear ahead at the zone, so A owes you mark-room for the whole rounding (Rule 18.2(a)) — her late overlap changes nothing. She is now leeward with right of way (Rule 11), but Rule 15 gives you room initially and Rule 17 caps her at her proper course. Take your seamanlike rounding; while sailing within your mark-room you're exonerated if she claims you didn't keep clear (Rule 43.1(b)).
answer.cost: If A squeezes you at the mark she breaks 18.2(a): Two-Turns Penalty on her.

title: Outside boat heats it up mid-rounding

q1.prompt: Overlapped at the zone, you inside, A outside to leeward. Mid-approach she starts luffing toward you. What do you do?

options.label: Respond to keep clear — but hold your mark-room
Ignore her — inside boat can't be luffed
Bail out behind her stern
Bear down on her to shut it down

answer.explanation: A is leeward with right of way (Rule 11), but you were overlapped inside at the zone, so she must give you mark-room (Rule 18.2(a)). When she changes course she must also give you room to keep clear (Rule 16.1). Respond to her luff — but she cannot luff you away from the space you need to round; inside that space you're protected (Rule 43.1(b)).
answer.cost: If her luff denies you a seamanlike rounding, she breaks 18.2(a)/16.1 and owes the turns — not you.

title: Rolled from astern on the run

q1.prompt: Mid-run, A surfs in from astern and gets an overlap a boat-width to leeward. What do you do?

options.label: Keep clear — she can't luff above proper course
Luff hard to defend — you kept right of way
Nothing — overtaking boat keeps clear
Gybe away immediately, forced out

answer.explanation: There's no 'overtaking boat keeps clear' rule — the moment A overlapped to leeward she took right of way (Rule 11). She must initially give you room (Rule 15), and because she came from clear astern within two lengths, Rule 17 pins her to her proper course — no luffing you. Keep clear, keep your air, and watch that she doesn't sail above it.
answer.cost: Sit on her and force a response: turns on you. If she luffs above proper course: turns on her (Rule 17).

title: Port boat tacks inside the zone

q1.prompt: You're fetching the mark on starboard, in the zone. A came in on port and tacked inside the zone, completing just ahead and to windward of you. What do you do?

options.label: Hold your course — she owes YOU room here
Bear away below her — she got there first
Shoot head to wind to avoid her
Tack away and come back

answer.explanation: She passed head to wind inside the zone coming from port, so Rule 18.3 applies: she must not cause you to sail above close-hauled, and because you're overlapped inside her, she must give you mark-room. You're also leeward, so you hold right of way (Rule 11). 'I got to the zone first' is worth nothing here — hold your lane and round inside.
answer.cost: If you have to luff above close-hauled or get squeezed at the mark, A owes a Two-Turns Penalty.

title: Leeward boat luffs — but she came from astern

q1.prompt: A overlapped you to leeward from astern earlier; now she's luffing, carrying you above the course to the mark. What do you do?

options.label: Keep clear — and protest the luff
Hold your course — her luff is illegal
Bear away into her to stop it
Follow her up as far as she likes, no foul

answer.explanation: A is leeward, so she has right of way (Rule 11) and you must keep clear even if her luff is illegal. But she overlapped from clear astern within two lengths, so Rule 17 forbids her sailing above her proper course — this luff breaks it, and while changing course she must give you room to respond (Rule 16.1). Keep clear first, protest after; ignoring her puts you in the wrong too.
answer.cost: Hold and make contact: you both get tossed. Keep clear and protest: turns on her.

title: Port layline: you're in the zone first

q1.prompt: You're on the port layline and entered the zone well before A, who's approaching on starboard. What do you do?

options.label: Duck her or tack — you have nothing
Claim mark-room — you reached the zone first
Hail 'room' and cross her bow
Pinch up and squeeze around ahead of her

answer.explanation: Rule 18 does not apply between boats on opposite tacks on a beat (Rule 18.1(a)), so reaching the zone first earns you nothing. This is a pure Rule 10 situation: port keeps clear of starboard, in the zone or not. Duck her stern or tack short — there is no room to claim.
answer.cost: Force her to alter: Two-Turns Penalty, and at a crowded mark that usually means clearing out entirely.

title: Opposite tacks at the leeward mark

q1.prompt: Running to the leeward mark: you're inside on port, A outside on starboard, overlapped at the zone. What do you do?

options.label: Take your mark-room — but don't take an inch more
Round however you like — inside boat rules
Gybe away — she's starboard, you're toast
Hail protest — she must gybe for you

answer.explanation: Downwind, boats on opposite tacks can be overlapped, and rule 18 applies at a leeward mark: overlapped at the zone means A must give you mark-room (Rule 18.2(a)) even though she holds right of way on starboard (Rule 10). Sailing within that room, you're exonerated for not keeping clear of her (Rule 43.1(b)). But the protection covers a seamanlike rounding only — swing wide and you're just a port-tack boat in the wrong.
answer.cost: Take more room than the rounding needs and you owe the turns, not her.

title: "I had the overlap!" — says the boat astern

q1.prompt: A, close astern, hails 'overlap!' as you reach the zone — you're sure she got it a beat too late, but it's close. What do you do?

options.label: Round normally — doubt goes against her claim
Give her the inside to be safe
Slam the door with a hard turn across her bow
Slow down and re-establish clear ahead

answer.explanation: What counts is the state when the first boat reaches the zone (Rule 18.2(a)) — and when there's reasonable doubt an overlap was gained in time, the rules presume it was not (Rule 18.2(e)). You were clear ahead: she keeps clear (Rule 12) and owes you mark-room. Round your normal line; no theatrics needed.
answer.cost: If she forces her bow in anyway, the penalty is hers — but avoid contact if she does (Rule 14).

title: The gap at the committee boat

q1.prompt: Final approach, 20 seconds to go. There's a gap between A (leeward of you) and the committee boat — you could just squeeze in. What do you do?

options.label: Bail out — slow down and drop in astern of A
Send it — she has to give you room at the mark
Hail for mark-room at the committee boat
Push in and let her protest

answer.explanation: There is no mark-room at a starting mark surrounded by navigable water — the Section C preamble switches rules 18–20 off from the moment you're approaching the line to start. You're just a windward boat under Rule 11, and A may luff to close that gap (giving you room to respond). Barging is a donation: kill your speed and tuck in astern.
answer.cost: If A has to bear away — or you hit her or the committee boat — the penalty (and the pile-up) is yours.

title: The overlap breaks inside the zone

q1.prompt: You were overlapped inside A at the zone; since then you've surged clear ahead of her. Does your mark-room survive?

options.label: Yes — round on your entitlement, she still gives room
No — the overlap broke, rights reset
Only if she agrees on the water
She gets the room now — she's astern and inside

answer.explanation: The obligation is set when the first boat reaches the zone and holds 'thereafter' (Rule 18.2(a)) — a broken overlap inside the zone doesn't reset it. You'd only lose the entitlement by leaving the zone or passing head to wind (Rule 18.2(b)). You're also now clear ahead, so she keeps clear under Rule 12 as well.
answer.cost: None for you — if she noses in at the mark now, the turns are hers.

title: You duck — she bears away at you

q1.prompt: You bear away to duck A's stern. She bears away too, keeping her bow on you. What do you do?

options.label: Keep ducking as best you can — she owes you room
Freeze your course and make her commit
Crash-tack out of there
Head back up across her bow

answer.explanation: She keeps right of way (Rule 10), and hunting is legal — but the moment she changes course she must give you room to keep clear (Rule 16.1). Keep reacting and keep clear; if her turn leaves you no way out, the foul is hers. Freezing up or cutting back across her bow just hands her the protest.
answer.cost: If you can't keep clear because of her turn: her penalty. If you stop reacting: yours.
